question: Will there be more than ten times as many 'Battles' in Tanzania for the 30 days before 2026-06-28 compared to one plus the 30-day average of 'Battles' over the 360 days preceding 2026-06-21? e.g. If the forecast due date is 2024-01-01 and we have the following data: Date,'Battles' 2023-11-11,1 2023-10-10,2 to calculate one plus the 30-day average of 'Battles' over the preceding 360 days, we’d have: 1+(1+2)/12=1.25. In this example, for the question to resolve positively, 13 (10 x 1.25) or more 'Battles' would need to occur in the 30 days leading up to the resolution. Resolution criteria: Resolves to the value calculated from the ACLED dataset once the data is published. Background: ACLED classifies events into six distinct categories: 1. Battles: violent interactions between two organized armed groups at a particular time and location; 2. Protests: in-person public demonstrations of three or more participants in which the participants do not engage in violence, though violence may be used against them; 3. Riots: violent events where demonstrators or mobs of three or more engage in violent or destructive acts, including but not limited to physical fights, rock throwing, property destruction, etc.; 4. Explosions/Remote violence: incidents in which one side uses weapon types that, by their nature, are at range and widely destructive; 5. Violence against civilians: violent events where an organized armed group inflicts violence upon unarmed non-combatants; and 6. Strategic developments: contextually important information regarding incidents and activities of groups that are not recorded as any of the other event types, yet may trigger future events or contribute to political dynamics within and across states. Detailed information about the categories can be found at: https://acleddata.com/knowledge-base/codebook/#acled-events
event_title:
Will there be more than ten times as many 'Battles' in Tanzania for the 30 days before 2026-06-28 compared to one plus the 30-day average of 'Battles' over the 360 days preceding 2026-06-21?

e.g. If the forecast due date is 2024-01-01 and we have the following data:
Date,'Battles'
2023-11-11,1
2023-10-10,2
to calculate one plus the 30-day average of 'Battles' over the preceding 360 days, we’d have: 1+(1+2)/12=1.25.

In this example, for the question to resolve positively, 13 (10 x 1.25) or more 'Battles' would need to occur in the 30 days leading up to the resolution.

Resolution criteria: Resolves to the value calculated from the ACLED dataset once the data is published.

Background: ACLED classifies events into six distinct categories:

1. Battles: violent interactions between two organized armed groups at a particular time and
   location;
2. Protests: in-person public demonstrations of three or more participants in which the participants
   do not engage in violence, though violence may be used against them;
3. Riots: violent events where demonstrators or mobs of three or more engage in violent or
   destructive acts, including but not limited to physical fights, rock throwing, property
   destruction, etc.;
4. Explosions/Remote violence: incidents in which one side uses weapon types that, by their nature,
   are at range and widely destructive;
5. Violence against civilians: violent events where an organized armed group inflicts violence upon
   unarmed non-combatants; and
6. Strategic developments: contextually important information regarding incidents and activities of
   groups that are not recorded as any of the other event types, yet may trigger future events or
   contribute to political dynamics within and across states.

Detailed information about the categories can be found at:
https://acleddata.com/knowledge-base/codebook/#acled-events

evidences.title: BTI 2026 Tanzania Country Report: BTI 2026
evidences.content: Anti-democratic actors Tanzania’s political leadership has sought to follow former President Nyerere’s direction on the issue of conflict management. It has continued to prevent cleavage-based conflicts from escalating. Notably, since President Hassan assumed office, no major or moderate cleavage-based conflict has emerged. There are no politically significant, deep-rooted ethnic, religious or separatist cleavages in Tanzania – with the exception of Zanzibar. Moderating conflict and integrating different interests have long characterized politics in Tanzania, sometimes at the expense of more substantial reforms. [...] At the union level, President Hassan’s conciliatory approach is likely to reduce political polarization and conflict, both of which were significant under her predecessor. However, doing so will require substantial reforms, including the abolishment of restrictive laws and the establishment of independent electoral commissions. [...] Civil society traditions Conflict intensity is very low. The population and political elite strongly reject violence as a means of political conflict. People of different religious, ethnic and socioeconomic backgrounds generally live p...

evidences.title: Africa Overview: June 2026 | ACLED
evidences.content: The campaign has affected several cercles in the Mopti region, including Bandiagara, Bankass, and Djenne. JNIM also carried out similar attacks in the neighboring Segou region. JNIM has targeted both militia positions and villages affiliated with Dozo militias through armed assaults and targeted killings. The group assassinated several militia members, including at least one local leader. The deadliest attacks were recorded in Gomossagou, Kori Kori, Dougara, Kourounde, and Bougoula. They drove a deadly spike in violence in the Mopti region in recent years. In the wake of the offensive, JNIM launched a disarmament campaign during which a dozen villages in the Bankass cercle handed over weapons to JNIM.7 The attacks also prompted criticism from affected communities and the Dogon civil [...] This is the first time Sudan and Ethiopia have publicly accused each other of supporting rival groups, escalating tensions between the two countries in connection with supporting such groups. Following this accusation, Sudan recalled its ambassador from Addis Ababa, and the SAF mobilized its forces near the Ethiopian border in Gedaref state. However, the risk of a direct war between Ethiopia an...

evidences.title: ACLED Regional Overview Africa: June 2026 - Democratic Republic of the Congo | ReliefWeb
evidences.content: ## Tanzania: The US sanctions a senior police officer The US State Department has designated Tanzania’s Senior Assistant Commissioner of Police Faustine Jackson Mafwele for “involvement in gross violations of human rights,” barring him from entering the United States.12 The notice cites his involvement in the detention, torture, and sexual assault of activists Agather Atuhaire and Boniface Mwangi one year ago.13 The pair had entered Tanzania to observe the trial of opposition leader Tundu Lissu, which is still ongoing. [...] This is the first time Sudan and Ethiopia have publicly accused each other of supporting rival groups, escalating tensions between the two countries in connection with supporting such groups. Following this accusation, Sudan recalled its ambassador from Addis Ababa, and the SAF mobilized its forces near the Ethiopian border in Gedaref state. However, the risk of a direct war between Ethiopia and Sudan remains low as both countries remain locked in domestic conflicts. Escalating instability within key volatile regions — specifically Ethiopia’s Tigray region and Sudan’s Blue Nile state — risks provoking intermittent border conflicts between the two countries....

evidences.title: [PDF] The Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project - ACLED
evidences.content: OVERALL TRENDS BY EVENT TYPE The overall decline in political violence is also driven by a 15% decrease in the total number of battles across ACLED’s coverage areas. By region, only Southeast Asia registered an increase in battle events, with ACLED recording a 14% rise. Every other form of political violence increased overall: explosions/remote violence, violence against civilians, and mob violence (a subset of riots) all rose from 2018 to 2019 (see Figure 4). - 14 -Figure 4. While ACLED records a 22% decrease in the number of fatalities stemming from battles, a 10% decrease in the number of fatalities stemming from explosions/remote violence, and a 4% decrease in the number of fatalities stemming from violence against civilians, the number of fatalities stemming from mob violence rose by [...] In all, disorder increased by 13% across all regions of ACLED coverage in 2019. ACLED 2019: The Year in Review explores these trends to better understand the myriad ways in which disorder manifests across countries and contexts. - 7 -TERMS In this report, the term political violence refers to all events coded with event type Battles, Explosions/Remote violence, and Violence against civili...

It helps organizations protect employees, secure assets, and adapt operations as risk rises or falls across locations. Country profiles show data at the subnational level, as well as trends based on number of events, fatalities, and civilians exposed to violence. Restricted page: Trends [...]...

evidences.title: Global conflict trends and 2026 predictions
evidences.content: ## ACLED's Post ### ACLED December 15, 2025 · 1 in 6 people was exposed to conflict in 2025 — How will conflict shift in 2026? Armed conflict is growing more severe. Governments and armed groups are using violence — often against civilians — with less restraints than seen in recent decades. Efforts to broker peace are stalling, and even when wars seem to ease somewhat, violence often returns and traps civilians in cycles of insecurity as the humanitarian system falters. ACLED’s Conflict Watchlist examines the current landscape and assesses prospects for escalation or resolution in the year ahead. Explore the 10 countries and regions projected to face armed conflict, political unrest, and humanitarian emergencies in 2026: No photo description available. evidences.title: Instagram
evidences.content: Tanzania’s Commission of Inquiry into the October 29, 2025 post-election unrest has identified five key root causes, citing political tensions, socio-economic pressures, governance failures, weak service delivery and international dynamics. Presenting the report at State House in Dar es Salaam on April 23, 2026, chairperson Retired Chief Justice Mohamed Chande Othman said the violence was not triggered by a single event but by long-standing grievances combined with immediate catalysts. The commission found that disputes over constitutional reforms, electoral credibility and political competition were central. Economic challenges, including the high cost of living, unemployment and tax concerns, also fuelled tensions.

evidences.title: Tanzania Country Security Report
evidences.content: Tanzania faces terrorist threats along three of its borders – with Kenya, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Mozambique. The Islamic State in Mozambique (IS-M) poses the greatest threat to Tanzania, having conducted more than a dozen attacks in Tanzania since 2020. These attacks have targeted civilians, including burning of villages, kidnapping, and looting of supplies, and have also targeted Tanzanian security services. As of the writing of this report, IS-M continues to be highly active in Mozambique along the border with Tanzania and the conflict continues to pose a threat to Tanzania. In response to growing IS-M activity, the Government of Tanzania sent additional security personnel to the border regions of Mtwara and Ruvuma as well as the neighboring Lindi region. [...] The U.S. Department of State has included a Terrorism “T” Indicator on the Travel Advisory for Tanzania, indicating that terrorist attacks have occurred and/or specific threats against civilians, groups, or other targets may exist. [...] No related content is available at this time. The contents of this (U) report in no way represent the policies, views, or attitudes of the United States Department of Sta...

evidences.content: This data is sourced using the Visual Crossing Weather API Visual Crossing provides hyper-local weather data and global forecasting APIs designed for developers, researchers, and enterprise teams. With 50+ years of historical data and industry-leading accuracy, we power smarter decisions across every industry.
evidences.query: Will there be more than ten times as many 'Battles' in Tanzania for the 30 days before 2026-06-28 compared to one plus the 30-day average of 'Battles' over the 360 days preceding 2026-06-21? e.g. If the forecast due date is 2024-01-01 and we have the following data: Date,'Battles' 2023-11-11,1 2023-10-10,2 to calculate one plus the 30-day average of 'Battles' over the preceding...
